attrs.alt: Reader role: what it can and cannot access

content.text: Reader is the least-privileged broad built-in role. Its Actions are ['*/read'], which matches the read operation registered by every Azure resource provider. A Reader can see every resource's existence, configuration, and metadata within their assigned scope. They cannot create, modify, or delete anything, and they cannot access the data stored inside resources (blob content, secrets, database rows — those require DataActions roles).

Reader is appropriate for security auditors who need visibility without write access, external consultants reviewing an architecture, monitoring agents that query resource state, and cost management tools that need to enumerate resources. At management group scope, a single Reader assignment provides read visibility across every subscription in the organization.

# Inspect the Reader role definition
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Reader' | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

# Key fields:
# Id          : acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7
# Actions     : [ '*/read' ]   <- only read operations on all providers
# NotActions  : []             <- no exclusions
# DataActions : []             <- no data plane access
# AssignableScopes: [ '/' ]

# What '*/read' means:
# Every resource provider registers a 'read' operation (GET on the ARM resource).
# '*/read' matches all of them: Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read,
# Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/read, Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/read, etc.

# What '*/read' does NOT match:
# - listKeys actions: Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listKeys/action
# - listSecrets: Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/listkeys/action
# - DataActions (completely separate evaluation)

# Verify by checking if Reader can list storage keys:
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Reader' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Actions
# Returns: [ '*/read' ]  — listKeys is an 'action', not 'read', so it is NOT included
Every read operation across all resource providers
No exclusions — all */read operations are included
No data plane access — blob/secret/row content excluded
Can be assigned at any scope in the tenant

Reader is safe to assign broadly because it carries no write risk. At subscription scope, a Reader can enumerate all resources in the subscription but cannot change anything. At management group scope, one assignment provides read coverage across all member subscriptions — ideal for security teams who need tenant-wide visibility.

# Assign Reader at subscription scope (read everything in the subscription)
$secTeam = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'Security Audit Team'"
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $secTeam.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

# Assign at management group scope (read everything across all member subscriptions)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $secTeam.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-mg-id>'

# Assign Reader at resource group scope for external consultant review
$consultant = Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'partner@external.com'"
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $consultant.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/audit-rg'

# List all current Reader assignments in the subscription
Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' |
    Select-Object DisplayName, ObjectType, Scope | Format-Table
Prefer group assignments over individual user assignments for team-wide Reader access.
Guest users with Reader at resource group scope is a common pattern for external architecture reviews — constrained and reversible.
Reader at subscription scope is the baseline for monitoring dashboards that query resource state via ARM APIs.
Reader does not grant access to Azure Portal's cost/billing views — those require Cost Management Reader additionally.

Reader's '*/read' pattern matches only operations named 'read'. Azure resource providers also define 'action' operations (like listKeys, listSecrets) that expose sensitive data and are distinct from read. A Reader cannot call any of these — they are not covered by the */read wildcard. This is intentional: storage account keys, Key Vault connection strings, and similar sensitive values require explicit action permissions.

# Reader cannot list storage account keys — it does NOT imply listKeys
# Trying to list keys as a Reader:
# Get-AzStorageAccountKey -ResourceGroupName 'data-rg' -Name 'proddata01'
# ERROR: The client '...' does not have authorization to perform action
# 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listKeys/action'

# To list keys, you need at least Storage Account Contributor
# (or a custom role with Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listkeys/action)

# Similarly, Reader cannot read the contents of a blob:
# Even with Reader on the storage account, accessing blob content via az storage blob
# with --auth-mode login will return 403 because that requires DataActions.

# Reader also cannot view connection strings for Service Bus, Event Hub, etc.
# These require listkeys actions:
# Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/authorizationRules/listkeys/action
# Microsoft.EventHub/namespaces/authorizationRules/listkeys/action

# To read diagnostics log content from a storage account:
# Reader on the account (sees the account exists, containers, metadata)
# +
# Storage Blob Data Reader (reads the blob bytes)
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId '<principal>' -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/logs-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/diaglogs'
Key Vault Reader (old model) or Key Vault Secrets User (RBAC model)

Reader provides resource metadata visibility but not observability data. Log Analytics workspace data, Application Insights telemetry, and alert histories require additional roles. The common pattern for SRE or operations teams is Reader (see the infrastructure) plus Monitoring Reader (query the observability data), optionally with Cost Management Reader for spending visibility.

# Reader + monitoring tools: ideal for observability-only access

# Reader lets you see:
# - Azure Monitor metric definitions and metric values via Get-AzMetric
# - Alert rule definitions
# - Diagnostic settings configuration
# But NOT the log content stored in a Log Analytics workspace

# To read Log Analytics query results, add Monitoring Reader
$monitoringGroup = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'SRE Team'"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $monitoringGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $monitoringGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Monitoring Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

# Monitoring Reader adds:
# - Read Log Analytics data (query workspace logs)
# - Read Application Insights data
# - Read alert history and activity log

# Cost management reader (read spending data without resource access)
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $financeGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Cost Management Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

# Verify effective permissions for an SRE team member at subscription scope
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'sre@contoso.com'").Id
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $userId -ExpandPrincipalGroups |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope | Format-Table

Security Reader is a role specifically designed for security operations teams. It includes all of Reader's permissions plus access to Microsoft Defender for Cloud data: alerts, recommendations, secure score, regulatory compliance, and policy compliance states. For SOC teams and security auditors who need to monitor the security posture across all subscriptions, Security Reader at root management group scope is the standard assignment.

# Security Reader is a superset of Reader for security-specific data
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Security Reader' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Actions

# Security Reader adds (over plain Reader):
# - Microsoft.Security/*
# - Microsoft.PolicyInsights/*/read
# - Microsoft.Authorization/*/read
# - Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/read
# This lets security teams read Defender for Cloud alerts, recommendations,
# secure score, regulatory compliance, and policy states across the tenant.

# Assign Security Reader to the SOC team at management group scope
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<soc-group-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Security Reader' `
  -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-mg-id>'

# Compare Reader vs Security Reader actions count
$reader = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Reader'
$secReader = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Security Reader'
Write-Host "Reader actions: $($reader.Actions.Count)"
Write-Host "Security Reader actions: $($secReader.Actions.Count)"

# Find all security-specific built-in roles (useful for selecting the right one)
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.Name -like '*Security*' -or $_.Name -like '*Defender*'
} | Select-Object Name, Description | Format-Table -AutoSize
Using Reader with Azure Resource Graph

Azure Resource Graph (ARG) is a query service that reads resource metadata from ARM at scale — across thousands of resources and multiple subscriptions in a single API call. Reader is sufficient for all ARG queries because ARG only accesses resource metadata (the same data that individual ARM read calls return). This makes Reader the right role for security posture scanning, compliance reporting, and infrastructure auditing at scale.

# Reader can use Azure Resource Graph for cross-subscription queries
# ARG lets you query resource metadata across all subscriptions in one call

# Find all VMs with no availability set (single point of failure)
Search-AzGraph -Query @"
Resources
| where type == 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| where isnull(properties.availabilitySet)
| project name, location, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
"@

# Count resources by type across all subscriptions (Reader on MG required)
Search-AzGraph -Query "Resources | summarize count() by type | order by count_ desc"

# Find storage accounts without HTTPS-only enforcement
Search-AzGraph -Query @"
Resources
| where type == 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts'
| where properties.supportsHttpsTrafficOnly == false
| project name, location, resourceGroup
"@

# Find all public IP addresses in use
Search-AzGraph -Query @"
Resources
| where type == 'microsoft.network/publicipaddresses'
| where isnotnull(properties.ipAddress)
| project name, location, resourceGroup, ipAddress = properties.ipAddress
"@

# Compute Reader can run all of these — ARG queries use ARM read operations only

Azure Resource Graph does not bypass RBAC. It only returns resources the caller has Reader access to. A query run at the management group level by a principal with Reader on that MG returns resources across all member subscriptions — the MG Reader assignment is inherited by every subscription and resource within it.

A common IaC pattern is to separate read-only 'plan' runs (during pull request review) from write 'apply' runs (on merge). The plan service principal needs only Reader — it calls data sources and computes the diff but makes no changes. The apply principal needs Contributor or a narrower custom role. This separation limits the blast radius of a compromised pipeline: a PR-time run with stolen credentials cannot modify any resources.

# Terraform: data sources are read-only and only require Reader
# Use Reader-level access for Terraform plan/drift-detection passes

# terraform data sources that work with Reader:
# data "azurerm_resource_group" "prod" { name = "prod-rg" }
# data "azurerm_virtual_network" "main" { name = "prod-vnet"; resource_group_name = "prod-rg" }
# data "azurerm_key_vault" "kv" { name = "prod-kv"; resource_group_name = "prod-rg" }
# data "azurerm_subscription" "current" {}

# A read-only 'plan' service principal: assign Reader at subscription scope
$tfPlanSp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId (New-MgApplication -DisplayName 'tf-plan-readonly').AppId
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $tfPlanSp.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>' -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal

# Separate apply service principal: assign Contributor for write operations
$tfApplySp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId (New-MgApplication -DisplayName 'tf-apply').AppId
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $tfApplySp.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg' -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal

# This separation means a PR-time terraform plan can run in CI with Reader only,
# while the apply step (merge to main) uses the more privileged Contributor SP.
Combining Reader with Cost and Billing Roles

Reader grants visibility over resource metadata but not over cost or billing data. Finance teams, budget owners, and cost optimization engineers typically need Reader (to understand what resources exist) combined with Cost Management Reader (to see spending data and forecasts). Billing Reader is a separate role scoped to the billing account level, not the subscription level.

# Cost visibility requires Cost Management Reader in addition to Reader
# Reader alone: can see resources exist, but billing/cost APIs return 403

# Assign both Reader and Cost Management Reader for finance team visibility
$financeGroup = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'Finance Team'"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $financeGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $financeGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Cost Management Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

# Cost Management Reader allows:
# - View actual and forecasted costs
# - View budgets and alerts
# - Export cost data
# But NOT: create budgets, modify exports (those require Contributor or Cost Management Contributor)

# Billing Reader (different from Cost Management Reader):
# - Used for Enterprise Agreement accounts
# - Grants access to billing account, profiles, invoice sections
# - Does NOT require Azure subscription scope — it's at billing account scope
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $financeGroup.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Billing Reader' `
    -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/<billing-account-id>'

# Verify cost data is visible after assignment
Get-AzConsumptionUsageDetail -StartDate (Get-Date).AddDays(-30) -EndDate (Get-Date) | Select-Object -First 5
Resource names, config, topology, ARM metadata
Log Analytics queries, Application Insights, metrics
Actual costs, forecasts, budgets (read), usage exports
Invoices, billing profiles, invoice sections (EA only)
Defender for Cloud alerts, secure score, compliance
Reader is Azure RBAC's safe, visibility-only role:
Actions: ['*/read'] — every resource's metadata and configuration, no writes, no data plane.
Does not include listKeys, listSecrets, or any 'action' type operation — sensitive data retrieval requires explicit roles.
DataActions are empty — blob content, Key Vault secrets, and database rows are not accessible.
Assign at management group scope for organization-wide visibility (security auditors, governance teams).
Combine with Monitoring Reader for log/metrics data and Cost Management Reader for billing views.
Security Reader is the right choice for SOC teams — it adds Defender for Cloud data on top of Reader's resource metadata.
